lookup_context: RVSM operations paragraph references aircraft COM, NAV, and approach equipment qualifiers
Do not change an equipment suffix when an ICAO equipment string amendment is needed
items.task: Identify the referenced lookup source for aircraft equipment qualifiers.
items.question_text: When RVSM equipment qualifiers need to be checked, which referenced table supplies aircraft COM, NAV, and approach equipment qualifiers?

choices.text: AIM TBL 5-1-4.

items.explanation: The RVSM paragraph references AIM TBL 5-1-4 for aircraft COM, NAV, and approach equipment qualifiers.

lookup_context: Operation: SFO/ELP or practice precautionary approach
Tower must obtain high-key altitude or maneuvering altitudes before approving the approach
Straight-in SFO final clearance normally no closer than 3 miles
items.task: Apply the figure-supported SFO/ELP sequencing requirements.
items.question_text: Before approving an SFO/ELP approach, what altitude information must be obtained from the aircraft concerned?

choices.text: The high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes.
Only the touchdown zone elevation.
Only the assigned cruise altitude.
No altitude information is needed if the aircraft reports initial.

items.explanation: The SFO/ELP paragraph requires obtaining the high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes before approving the approach.

lookup_context: Radar application paragraph references aircraft equipment suffixes
Relevant to RNAV/GNSS capability context
items.task: Identify the referenced table used for equipment suffix support.
items.instruction: Use the aircraft equipment suffix reference.
items.question_text: Which referenced table supports aircraft equipment suffix lookup in the radar-application context?

choices.text: TBL 2-3-10, Aircraft Equipment Suffixes.
TBL 5-5-2, Wake Turbulence Separation for On Approach.
TBL 8-7-1, ADS-C Criteria.
TBL 14-2-1, CPDLC Response Attribute.

items.explanation: The radar application paragraph references FAA Order JO 7110.65, TBL 2-3-10, Aircraft Equipment Suffixes.

lookup_context: Parallel runway thresholds are even
Question: required runway centerline spacing for departures/arrivals on parallel or nonintersecting diverging runways
items.task: Choose the centerline spacing minimum for the even-threshold case.
items.question_text: When parallel runway thresholds are even, what minimum runway centerline spacing in feet is required for the 5-8-5 parallel runway case?

choices.text: At least 2,500 feet.
At least 1,000 feet.
At least 700 feet.
At least 500 feet.

items.explanation: For even parallel runway thresholds, 5-8-5 requires runway centerlines at least 2,500 feet apart.

lookup_context: Longitudinal separation methods
Same tracks, crossing tracks, and reciprocal tracks use different geometry
items.task: Choose the measurement concept for crossing tracks.
items.instruction: Use the offshore/oceanic longitudinal separation figures.
items.question_text: For crossing tracks in the 8-3-2 figures, where is the applicable longitudinal spacing evaluated?

choices.text: At the point of intersection.
Only at the departure point.
Only at the oceanic boundary.
At the point where both aircraft report the same Mach number.

items.explanation: For crossing tracks, the estimated spacing is evaluated at the point of intersection.

lookup_context: Lateral separation methods
Nonintersecting and intersecting flight paths use different geometry tests
items.task: Identify the lateral separation test for intersecting flight paths with constant and same width protected airspace.
items.instruction: Use the lateral separation method figures.
items.question_text: For intersecting flight paths with constant and same-width protected airspace, what does the 8-4-2 figure logic use to establish lateral separation?

choices.text: Either aircraft at or beyond the applicable lateral minimum measured perpendicular to the other flight path.
Only the distance from the departure airport.
Only vertical separation before and after estimated passing.
The ADS-C periodic reporting interval.

items.explanation: For that intersecting-path case, separation exists when either aircraft is at or beyond the applicable lateral separation minimum measured perpendicular to the other flight path.

lookup_context: Altitude: below FL240
Reduced route protected airspace
Initial width measured each side of route centerline
items.task: Choose the reduced protected width and distance from the NAVAID before expansion begins.
items.instruction: Apply the reduced route protected airspace rule.
items.question_text: Below FL240, what reduced protected width applies on each side of route centerline before widening begins, and to what distance in miles from the NAVAID?

choices.text: 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
10 miles each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
4 miles each side to 51 miles from the NAVAID.
50 miles each side immediately from the NAVAID.

items.explanation: Below FL240, protected airspace may be reduced to 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then widened on a 5-degree angle.

lookup_context: Aircraft crossing offshore/oceanic boundary below FL180
Vertical separation is being applied
QNE/QNH differences may affect separation
items.task: Identify what control action must account for.
items.instruction: Use the altimeter setting transition figure.
items.question_text: When vertical separation is applied below FL180 across the offshore/oceanic boundary, what risk does FIG 8-5-1 highlight?

choices.text: Differences between standard altimeter setting and local altimeter setting must not compromise separation.
Mach number technique must be discontinued at the boundary.
CPDLC must replace voice at the boundary.
Wake turbulence spacing must be doubled below FL180.

items.explanation: The figure supports the requirement to account for standard versus local altimeter setting differences so vertical separation is not compromised.

lookup_context: Transition from offshore to oceanic airspace
Outbound aircraft climbs through opposite-direction oceanic traffic
items.task: Choose when vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic must be established.
items.question_text: For an offshore-to-oceanic opposite-direction transition, how many minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass must vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic be established?

choices.text: 15 minutes.
10 minutes.
5 minutes.
2 minutes.

items.explanation: Vertical separation above the opposite-direction oceanic traffic must be established before the boundary crossing and 15 minutes before estimated passing.

lookup_context: Same-direction offshore/oceanic transition
Following aircraft must remain in direct communication until vertical separation is established
items.task: Identify the communication condition shown by the same-direction transition figure.
items.question_text: In the same-direction offshore/oceanic transition case, what communication condition remains required until vertical separation is established?

choices.text: The following aircraft must remain in direct communication with ATC.
The leading aircraft must switch to advisory frequency.
Both aircraft must use CPDLC only.
Communication may be discontinued once the aircraft cross the boundary.

items.explanation: For the same-direction transition case, the following aircraft must remain in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.

lookup_context: Temporary stationary airspace reservation
Two methods: lateral route protection or vertical separation with buffer
items.task: Choose the lateral method shown by the figure.
items.instruction: Use the temporary stationary reservation figures.
items.question_text: For lateral separation from a temporary stationary reservation, what must be true of the aircraft route's protected airspace?

choices.text: It must not overlap the geographical area of the stationary reservation.
It may overlap if the aircraft is above FL240.
It must cross the reservation at a right angle.
It only needs to avoid the reservation center point.

items.explanation: The lateral method clears aircraft so protected airspace along the route does not overlap the geographical area of the stationary reservation.

lookup_context: Unmanned free balloon
Figure depicts the shape of an 11 million cubic feet balloon at various altitudes
Payload descent by parachute is normally at least 1,000 feet per minute
items.task: Identify what the figure contributes operationally.
items.question_text: What does FIG 9-6-1 help ATC anticipate about an 11 million cubic feet unmanned free balloon?

choices.text: Its physical shape changes at various altitudes or flight levels.
Its CPDLC response attribute changes by altitude.
It must fly only published RNAV routes.
It descends at the same rate as powered aircraft.

items.explanation: FIG 9-6-1 depicts how the physical shape of a large unmanned free balloon changes at various altitudes.

lookup_context: DCL/PDC/CPDLC selectable departure clearance application
Domestic en route CPDLC message set is table-defined
items.task: Identify where the domestic en route CPDLC messages are contained.
items.instruction: Use the CPDLC message table range.
items.question_text: Where are the domestic en route CPDLC messages identified for DCL/PDC/CPDLC use?

choices.text: TBL 14-2-1 through TBL 14-2-23.
TBL 13-1-1 through TBL 13-1-3.
TBL 8-7-1 through TBL 8-10-1.
TBL 2-4-1 only.

items.explanation: The DCL/PDC/CPDLC material points to TBL 14-2-1 through TBL 14-2-23 for domestic en route CPDLC messages.
